titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Inez Smith Reid papers
titlestmt.author: Elizabeth Nosari

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

conventiondeclaration.citation: translation missing: en.enumerations.resource_finding_aid_description_rules.Describing Archives: A Content Standard

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Tuesday June 10, 2025 at 23:00

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Chiefly in English. Some material in French.

did.abstract: This collection documents the life and career of Inez Smith Reid, particularly her writing, research, and advocacy work during her academic and legal career prior to her appointment to a judgeship on the District of Columbia Court of Appeals in 1995. It includes personal papers in the form of scrapbook materials, student writing, awards and certificates, and photographs. The collection also contains writings such as research for her 1972 book "Together" Black Women , academic publications, and speech typescripts, manuscripts, and related ephemera. Also present are subject files composed of Smith Reid's research interests in nascent Black studies programs, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Black women, and Brooklyn College student arrests (1968-1970), among others. Smith Reid's academic and community development work is also documented in the collection, including for the African Heritage Studies Association (AHSA) and the Black Women's Community Development Foundation (BWCDF).

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

Box 22 (student records): Restricted until 2044. For further information contact Access Services, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library. Box 23 (born digital material/DVD-R storage): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: This collection documents the life and career of Inez Smith Reid. Personal papers, 1955–2005, include early writings and speeches; dissertations from Columbia University, 1968, and the University of Virginia, 2004; scrapbook pages and materials, 1955–1988; and photographs, circa 1970s–circa 1990s. Writings, 1963–2019, consist of print publications, typescripts, research, and notes from Smith Reid's 1972 book "Together" Black Women in addition to journal articles, papers, reports, book reviews, a monograph, and a proposal. Much of this series relates to Smith Reid's speeches and panel participation and includes typescripts, notes, ephemera, and a DVD-R. Subject files, 1959–circa 2004, represent Smith Reid's research interests in Black studies, African studies (particularly the Democratic Republic of the Congo), law, political science, Black women, and civil rights, among other topics. Academic and community development work, 1968–1982, contains materials related to the Black Women's Community Development Foundation (BWCDF), 1972–1976; African Heritage Studies Association (AHSA), 1969–1975; Ad Hoc Committee on an Institute of Afro-American and Puerto Rican Studies at Brooklyn College, New York, New York, circa 1968–circa 1970; Antioch College, Yellow Springs, Ohio, 1975–1985; and the Ford Foundation, 1971. This series includes correspondence, meeting minutes, newsletters, ephemera, research, and notes.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Julia P. Cooper Mack papers (GEN MSS 2155). Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: During processing in May 2025, 1 box of Julia P. Cooper Mack's papers was removed from the Inez Smith Reid papers in order to create the Julia P. Cooper Mack papers (GEN MSS 2155).

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Most folder titles have been transcribed directly from Inez Smith Reid's original folders. Devised folder titles and additions to existing folder titles have been enclosed in brackets.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized in four series: I. Personal papers, 1955–2005. II. Writings, 1963–2019. III. Subject files, 1959–circa 2004. IV. Academic and community development work, 1968–1982.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Inez Smith Reid (née Elsie Inez Virginia Smith) was born on April 7, 1937, in New Orleans, Louisiana, to Beatrice Bundy and Sidney R. Smith, Sr. Her twin brother was lawyer and judge George Bundy Smith (1937–2017). Smith Reid grew up in segregation in Washington, D.C., and graduated from Dunbar High School, the first Black public secondary school in the United States. She attended Howard University, 1955–1956, before transferring and earning a BA in sociology, magna cum laude, from Tufts University in 1959. She earned an LLB from Yale Law School in 1962; an MA in Political Science from the University of California, Los Angeles, in 1963; a PhD in Public Law and Government from Columbia University in 1968; and an LLM from the University of Virginia School of Law in 2004. Smith Reid made professional contributions in the fields of academia, government service, activism, and private law before her appointment to the District of Columbia Court of Appeals in 1995.

Smith Reid began her career as a professor. She was lecturer in criminal law and law librarian at École Nationale de Droit et d'Administration in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, 1963–1964, a position funded by Yale Law School, the Ford Foundation, and the Congolese government. She then held teaching positions in New York, including Assistant Professor of African Studies and Political Science at State University of New York, New-Paltz, 1964–1965; lecturer in Political Science at Hunter College, City University of New York, 1965–1966; Instructor/Assistant Professor/Associate Professor of Political Science at Brooklyn College, City University of New York, 1969–1974; and Associate Professor of Political Science at Barnard College, 1971–1976. She returned to teaching later in her career as William J. Maier, Jr., Visiting Professor to University of West Virginia, College of Law, 1985–1986, and Adjunct Professor of Government and Constitutional Scholar at American University, 1989–2006.

Smith Reid contributed to a number of academic, civil rights, and community development organizations over the course of her career, including serving as legal researcher and lawyer at the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund during the summers of 1963 and 1964. She was also a member of the Executive Board/Executive Advisory Committee of African Heritage Studies Association, circa 1969–circa 1975, and the Ad Hoc Committee on an Institute of Afro-American and Puerto Rican Studies at Brooklyn College, New York, New York, circa 1968–circa 1970. She served as Executive Director at the Black Women's Community Development Foundation, Washington, DC, circa 1972–circa 1976. At Tufts University, she was a member of the Board of Trustees, 1988–1998, and the Board of Advisors, Arts and Sciences, 2011–2020; she has served as a Trustee Emeritus since 1999.

Smith Reid also held federal and state government positions during her career. She was general counsel for New York State Executive Department, State Division for Youth, 1976–1977. In Washington, D.C., she was deputy general counsel for regulation review for the U.S. Department of Health, Education, and Welfare; Inspector General for the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, 1979–1981; and Corporation Counsel of Washington, D.C., 1983–1985. She also worked in private practice at Washington, Perito & Dubac, 1986–1991, Graham & James, 1991–1994, and Lewis, White & Clay, 1994–1995.

Smith Reid was appointed as Associate Judge to the District of Columbia Court of Appeals in 1995 by President Bill Clinton, and she later served as Senior Judge, 2011–2017, on the same court. She has been recognized for her professional achievements, including through the Emily Gregory Award from Barnard College in 1976; Pro Bono Award from the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund in 1990; the Hero in Law Award from the Olender Foundation in 2008; Stars of the Bar Award Recipient from the Women's Bar Association of the District of Columbia in 2011; Charles Hamilton Houston Medallion of Merit from the Washington Bar Association in 2012; and the Dean's Medal from Tufts University School of Arts and Sciences in 2019, among others. She was also presented with an Honorary Doctor of Laws degree from Tuskagee University in Alabama in 1995.
Smith Reid was married to Frantz Francois-Joseph Reid from 1964 to 1990.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Inez Smith Reid Papers. General Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Inez Smith Reid papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains early writings and speeches from the 1950s and 1960s as well as Smith Reid's 1968 Columbia University dissertation "Constitutional Development in the Democratic Republic of the Congo" and her 2004 University of Virginia thesis "Historical Links: The Remarkable Legacy and Legal Journey of the Hon. Julia Cooper Mack." It also includes scrapbook pages containing memorabilia including correspondence, event ephemera, press, and publications. Also present are photographs, circa 1970s–circa 1990s, of Smith Reid in addition to awards and certificates.

did.unittitle: [Columbia University dissertation - "Constitutional Development in the Democratic Republic of the Congo"]
[University of Virginia thesis - "Historical Links: The Remarkable Legacy and Legal Journey of the Hon. Julia Cooper Mack"]

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized in three subseries and chronologically therein: "Together" Black Women, 1970–circa 1975. Articles, Proposals, Book Reviews, and Reports, 1963–1979. Speeches and Panel Discussions, 1969–2019.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series is comprised of materials relating to Smith Reid's book "Together" Black Women (1972), including anonymized interviews, notes, research, a copy of the publication, and materials related to talks and discussions about the book. It is also comprised of journal articles related to Smith Reid's academic interests in law, Black studies, African studies, and political science, including "The Evolution of a New Nation: Problems of Judicial Reorganization" in Southern California Law Review , 1964; "An Analysis of Black Studies Programs" in Afro-American Studies , 1970; and "Science, Politics, and Race" in SIGNS , 1975, among others. Also present are papers, reports, and book reviews as well as a monograph and proposal.

Additionally, this series documents Smith Reid's speeches and participation in panel discussions through typescripts and holograph manuscripts of prepared remarks in addition to research, notes, and related event ephemera. In addition to talks at universities, conferences, and churches, including the People's Congregational United Church of Christ, Washington, D.C., a number of Smith Reid's award acceptance speeches are represented, including the Emily Gregory Award from Barnard College in 1976; her Honorary Doctor of Laws degree from Tuskagee University in 1995; and the Charles Hamilton Houston Medallion of Merit from the Washington Bar Association in 2012.

accessrestrict.p: Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

Peoples Congregational United Church of Christ - Girl Scout Sunday Address

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains research material pertaining to Smith Reid's professional academic interests in subject areas including the constitution and legal landscape of the Democratic Republic of the Congo, 1959–1966; Black studies, 1969–1971, particularly in relation to her committee work for Brooklyn College; African current events, including drought and famine, 1973–1974; student demonstrations at Brooklyn College and Franklin K. Lane High School in New York City, 1968–1969; and the political campaigns of George Bundy Smith, 1975, and Harold A. Stevens,1974. Also present are research files relating to Smith Reid's writings, including "Optimism Is Not Warranted," circa 1977; "How Powerful Is Powerful?," 1976–1977, and related topics such as the environment, minorities, poverty, Julia Cooper Mack, and US and African politics. Record types include news clippings, newspapers, periodicals, reports, notes (some in French), correspondence, and related ephemera.

did.unittitle: [African current events publications from Zaire (Democratic Republic of the Congo) and more - in French and English]

SEEK [Search for Education, Elevation and Knowledge] - Queen's College
Series IV: Academic and community development work

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series documents Smith Reid's academic and community development work. Materials related to the Black Women's Community Development Foundation (BWCDF), 1972–1976, where Smith Reid served as Executive Director, include correspondence (some in French), notes, press clippings, meeting minutes, a 1972 annual report, and pamphlets. In addition, BWCDF programming materials include a 1974 Sojourner Truth Awards program; a transcript of proceedings of a "Miniconsultation of the Mental and Physical Health of Black Women" in 1974; and programs related to a Symposium on Black Women in 1972. Also present are materials relating to the BCDF's work on community development initiatives in Africa's Sahel region, 1974 to 1976, including proposals, correspondence, reports, research, notes, project design, and materials related to travel.

In addition, this series documents Smith Reid's involvement with the African Heritage Studies Association (AHSA), 1969–1975, as a member of the Executive Committee. It includes organizational correspondence (some in French) as well as correspondence, memos, and educational materials by historian and AHSA president John Henrik Clarke (1915–1998). Also present are financial statements, press clippings, conference ephemera, resolutions, newsletters, and meeting materials (e.g., lists of registrants, agendas, and minutes) in addition to reports and guidance on African studies curricula.

This series also contains materials related to Smith Reid's service as a member of the Ad Hoc Committee on an Institute of Afro-American and Puerto Rican Studies at Brooklyn College, New York, New York, circa 1968–circa 1970, during her tenure as professor of Political Science. Materials include correspondence, proposals, reports, and programming materials. Also present is correspondence relating to Smith Reid's work with Antioch College, Yellow Springs, Ohio, where she was a member of the Board of Trustees, 1975–1985, and Ford Foundation materials, including a letter of appointment as a consultant to the Ford Foundation's Office for the Middle East and Africa in 1971.

did.unittitle: Afro-American Institute Committee and Committee on an Institute of Afro-American and Puerto Rico Studies, Brooklyn College, CUNY
